title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Owen Lee, who is a landscape photographer.
audio_script: Interviewer: Owen Lee is a landscape photographer whose pictures have appeared in several magazines. Owen, were you always interested in photography? || Guest: I liked taking holiday photos, but nothing serious. I studied geography, and during a field trip our teacher asked us to record changes in the coast. [20]I found that taking pictures helped me notice the shape of the land more carefully[/20], and that was the beginning. || Interviewer: What was your first professional job like? || Guest: It was for a walking magazine. I had to photograph a mountain path in spring. I was nervous about the camera, but that was fine. [21]The hardest thing was waiting for the right light while feeling cold and hungry[/21]. Good pictures take patience. || Interviewer: Do you prefer working alone? || Guest: Sometimes. Being alone helps me concentrate. But I also run workshops now. [22]I enjoy showing beginners that they can take better photos just by moving a few steps or waiting a minute[/22]. They don't always need expensive cameras. || Interviewer: Has technology changed your work? || Guest: Definitely. Drones can produce amazing views, and phones are much better now. Still, [23]I spend less time worrying about equipment than about choosing the best place to stand[/23]. That's what makes a picture strong. || Interviewer: What do you dislike about the job? || Guest: People think bad weather is the problem, but it can make dramatic pictures. [24]What I dislike is when visitors damage places by climbing fences or walking on protected plants to copy a photo[/24]. That really annoys me. || Interviewer: What's your next project? || Guest: I've photographed mountains for years, so now I want a change. [25]I'm planning a book about ordinary green spaces in towns, like small parks and river paths[/25]. They can be beautiful too. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Owen, many people take photos on their phones now. Does that make your work harder? || Guest: I don't think so. It means more people are interested in images, and that is positive. A phone can take a very good picture if the person using it looks carefully. Professional work is different because clients need a particular result in all kinds of conditions, but the basic skill is the same: noticing what is in front of you. || Interviewer: What do you do when the weather is terrible for several days? || Guest: I change my plan. If the sky is flat and grey, I look for details such as walls, leaves, water or shapes in paths. Some of my favourite pictures were taken when I had stopped waiting for a perfect sunset. Bad weather can force you to be more creative. || Interviewer: Do you print your photographs? || Guest: Yes, and I think everyone should print a few. Pictures on a screen disappear too quickly. When I print a photo, I see mistakes I missed before, but I also enjoy it more. Holding the final image makes the work feel real, not just like another file on a computer. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Owen Lee is a landscape photographer whose pictures have appeared in several magazines. Owen, were you always interested in photography?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I liked taking holiday photos, but nothing serious. I studied geography, and during a field trip our teacher asked us to record changes in the coast. I found that taking pictures helped me notice the shape of the land more carefully, and that was the beginning.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was your first professional job like?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It was for a walking magazine. I had to photograph a mountain path in spring. I was nervous about the camera, but that was fine. The hardest thing was waiting for the right light while feeling cold and hungry. Good pictures take patience.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you prefer working alone?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Sometimes. Being alone helps me concentrate. But I also run workshops now. I enjoy showing beginners that they can take better photos just by moving a few steps or waiting a minute. They don't always need expensive cameras.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has technology changed your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Definitely. Drones can produce amazing views, and phones are much better now. Still, I spend less time worrying about equipment than about choosing the best place to stand. That's what makes a picture strong.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you dislike about the job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People think bad weather is the problem, but it can make dramatic pictures. What I dislike is when visitors damage places by climbing fences or walking on protected plants to copy a photo. That really annoys me.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What's your next project?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I've photographed mountains for years, so now I want a change. I'm planning a book about ordinary green spaces in towns, like small parks and river paths. They can be beautiful too.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Owen, many people take photos on their phones now. Does that make your work harder?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I don't think so. It means more people are interested in images, and that is positive. A phone can take a very good picture if the person using it looks carefully. Professional work is different because clients need a particular result in all kinds of conditions, but the basic skill is the same: noticing what is in front of you.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you do when the weather is terrible for several days?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I change my plan. If the sky is flat and grey, I look for details such as walls, leaves, water or shapes in paths. Some of my favourite pictures were taken when I had stopped waiting for a perfect sunset. Bad weather can force you to be more creative.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you print your photographs?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, and I think everyone should print a few. Pictures on a screen disappear too quickly. When I print a photo, I see mistakes I missed before, but I also enjoy it more. Holding the final image makes the work feel real, not just like another file on a computer.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: How did Owen first become seriously interested in photography?
questions.explanation: Owen says a geography field trip made him notice land through taking photographs.

questions.question: What was difficult about Owen's first professional job?
questions.explanation: He says the hardest thing was waiting for the right light while cold and hungry.

questions.question: What does Owen enjoy about teaching beginners?

options.B: showing them simple ways to improve

questions.explanation: Owen says he enjoys showing beginners that small changes can improve their photos.

questions.question: Owen thinks the most important thing in photography is
questions.explanation: He says he worries more about choosing the best place to stand than about equipment.

questions.question: What annoys Owen when he is working?

options.C: visitors asking to copy his photos

questions.explanation: Owen dislikes visitors damaging places by climbing fences or walking on protected plants.

questions.question: What is Owen planning to photograph next?
questions.explanation: He says he is planning a book about ordinary green spaces in towns.
